ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated breadcrumbs code; A000E (bread and similar products) is the closest defensible parent category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for processed breadcrumbs; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of breadcrumbs used as a coating or topping is approximately 15 g (about 2 tablespoons), providing roughly 57 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Breadcrumbs, dry, grated, plain — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168003)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for plain dry grated breadcrumbs is an exact match for this generic processed breadcrumb product. HPB Singapore values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol are preserved from the input record (data_source: hpb, confidence: 0.95) and are consistent with USDA SR Legacy values. Saturated fat sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 168003.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 75 for white bread / plain breadcrumbs is consistent with values reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Plain dried breadcrumbs are derived from white bread and carry an equivalent high GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (75 × 10.275 g available carbohydrate per 15 g serving) / 100 = 7.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 650 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g high-concern threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should avoid or strictly limit plain commercial breadcrumbs (650 mg sodium/100 g) and instead use low-sodium or homemade breadcrumbs, or substitute with ground oats or wholegrain panko with verified low-sodium labelling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (75) and high available carbohydrate (68.5 g/100 g) with moderate fibre (4.7 g/100 g) producing a significant glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should use plain breadcrumbs only in small coating quantities (≤15 g per serving) to limit glycemic load, and should prefer wholegrain or almond-flour-based breadcrumbs with a lower GI to reduce postprandial blood glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (650 mg/100 g) raises blood pressure risk; saturated fat is low (0.9 g/100 g) and cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g, limiting direct lipid concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, the primary concern with breadcrumbs is the high sodium content (650 mg/100 g), which can contribute to hypertension and adverse cardiac outcomes; use sparingly as a coating only and select low-sodium alternatives where available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Breadcrumbs are a processed grain product made by drying and grinding bread — typically white wheat bread — into fine, golden-brown particles. They have a dry, crispy texture and a mild toasted wheat flavour. Commercially produced plain breadcrumbs are used extensively as a coating for fried or baked foods (e.g., schnitzel, fish fillets, croquettes), as a binder in meatballs and patties, and as a crunchy topping for gratins and casseroles. Per 100 g, breadcrumbs provide 382 kcal, 14 g protein, 4.1 g fat (0.9 g saturated), 68.5 g carbohydrate, 4.7 g dietary fibre, 4.6 g sugar, and 650 mg sodium. The high sodium content (650 mg/100 g) makes them unsuitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management without careful portion control. The high glycemic index (approximately 75) and high carbohydrate density make them a high-concern food for Type 2 Diabetes. They contain gluten and wheat allergens and are not suitable for coeliac or gluten-intolerant individuals. Wholegrain, panko, or almond-flour-based alternatives offer lower GI and reduced sodium options.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of breadcrumbs?
What can I use instead of breadcrumbs?
How much protein is in breadcrumbs?
meal_context: topping for gratins and baked dishes

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore (confidence 0.95) and corroborated by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168003) for plain dry grated breadcrumbs — an exact food match. Saturated fat supplemented from USDA SR Legacy. GI value of 75 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database values for white bread derivatives.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Singapore source for this entry; USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 168003 provides partial coverage but values were not individually confirmed against the HPB record and are therefore conservatively set to -1 pending source reconciliation. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100 g — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore source (confidence 0.95); breadcrumbs are a plant-derived grain product containing no animal-derived cholesterol by formulation. Glycemic load per serving calculated at 15 g serving size: (75 × 10.275) / 100 = 7.7. The food is a generic Western processed grain product with no mandatory multilingual naming requirement under Project Veritas v2 rules; local_names fields are null and aliases_by_language non-English arrays are empty, which is compliant for this cuisine category.
